AI Agent Flow:

User Question → Agent → Selects Tool → Executes Tool →

  Gets Result → Reasons → Returns Answer

LangChain Components:

  1. LLM: The AI brain (GPT-4, etc.)
  2. Tools: Functions the agent can call
  3. Prompt: Instructions for the agent
  4. Agent: Decision-maker that uses tools
  5. Executor: Runs the agent loop

FastAPI Flow:

HTTP Request → FastAPI Route → Python Function →

  Process → Return Response

Common Pitfalls to Avoid

  1. Forgetting to activate virtual environment
    • Always see (venv) in your terminal
  2. Tool descriptions too vague
    • Bad: "Gets data"
    • Good: "Retrieves quality issues for batches with Open or Escalated status"
  3. Not handling errors
    • Always use try-except when calling external APIs
  4. Running wrong Python version
    • Use python --version to check

What You've Learned

In Part 2, you now understand:

✅ What Python, FastAPI, and LangChain are
✅ How AI agents make decisions and use tools
✅ How to structure a Python project
✅ How to connect to the SAP CAP backend
✅ The basics of building FastAPI endpoints

Next Steps

In Part 3, we'll combine everything to build the complete AI agent service that:

  • Detects quality issues automatically
  • Generates mitigation plans
  • Executes corrective actions
  • Integrates with SAP AI Core

Resources for Learning More

Python Basics:

  • Python.org Official Tutorial
  • Real Python

FastAPI:

  • FastAPI Documentation
  • FastAPI Tutorial

LangChain:

  • LangChain Documentation
  • LangChain Agents Guide

Continue to Part 3: Building the Complete AI Agent Service

entry.title: AI-Powered Chemical Quality Management System: Part 3 - Creating AI Agent with LangChain & FastAPI
entry.content:
Introduction

Welcome to Part 3! You've built the SAP CAP backend (Part 1) and learned Python, FastAPI, and LangChain fundamentals (Part 2). Now we'll implement the complete AI agent using the exact code structure from your project.

What we'll build:

  • Three specialized tools that interact with the CAP backend
  • An intelligent LangChain agent powered by SAP AI Core
  • FastAPI endpoints for natural language queries
  • Complete integration between all components

By the end, your system will:

  • Detect quality issues through natural language queries
  • Generate detailed mitigation plans
  • Execute corrective actions automatically

Architecture Overview

User Query (Natural Language)

    ↓

FastAPI Endpoint (/qualityIssue, /mitigationPlan, /executeMitigationPlan)

    ↓

LangChain Agent (llm_agent.py)

    ↓

Agent Executor decides which tool to use

    ↓

Tool (chemical_service.py) calls CAP Backend via OData

    ↓

Process results and return to Agent

    ↓

Agent formulates natural language response

    ↓

Return JSON response to user

Project Structure

Let's organize our Python agent service properly:

chemical-quality-agent/

 

├── venv/                        # Python virtual environment

├── ai_core.json                 # SAP AI Core credentials

├── requirements.txt             # Python dependencies

├── chemical_service.py          # LangChain tools for CAP backend

├── llm_agent.py                 # Agent configuration with AI Core

└── main.py                      # FastAPI application

Step 1: Understanding the Complete Code

Before we dive into implementation, let's understand what each file does:

chemical_service.py: Contains three LangChain tools (decorated with @tool) that make HTTP requests to our CAP backend OData service.

llm_agent.py: Configures the connection to SAP AI Core, initializes the LLM (GPT-4o-mini), creates the agent with tools, and sets up the agent executor.

main.py: Creates FastAPI endpoints that accept natural language queries and pass them to the agent executor.

Step 2: Creating the Tools (chemical_service.py)

Tools are Python functions that the AI agent can call. Each tool must have a clear description (docstring) that tells the agent when to use it.

File: chemical_service.py

from langchain.tools import tool

import requests

 

BASE_URL = "http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/chemical"

 

@tool

def get_products_with_quality_issues(input: str) -> str:

    """

    Get all product batches with quality issues (Open or Escalated).

    """

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/QualityIssue?$expand=batch&$filter=status eq 'Open' or status eq 'Escalated'"

    try:

        response = requests.get(url)

        if response.ok:

            issues = response.json().get("value", [])

            if not issues:

                return "No open or escalated quality issues found."

            result = " ".join([

                f"Product ID: {issue['batch']['productID']}, Issue: {issue['issueType']}, Severity: {issue.get('severity', 'N/A')}"

                for issue in issues

            ])

            return result

        return f"Error fetching issues: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"

    except Exception as e:

        return f"Exception occurred: {e}"

 

 

@tool

def get_mitigation_plan(input: str) -> str:

    """

    Fetch mitigation plans for product batches with quality issues.

    Filters based on 'Open' or 'Escalated' statuses and provides structured suggestions.

    """

    try:

        response = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/RecommendationView")

        if response.ok:

            recommendations = response.json().get("value", [])

            filtered = [rec for rec in recommendations if rec["status"] in ["Open", "Escalated"]]

 

            if not filtered:

                return "No open or escalated mitigation plans found."

 

            result_lines = []

            grouped = {}

            for rec in filtered:

                product = rec["productID"]

                if product not in grouped:

                    grouped[product] = {

                        "severity": rec["severity"],

                        "suggestions": []

                    }

                grouped[product]["suggestions"].append({

                    "suggestion": rec["suggestion"],

                    "workflow": rec["triggeredWorkflowID"],

                    "status": rec["status"]

                })

 

            for product, details in grouped.items():

                result_lines.append(f"### {product} (Severity: {details['severity']})")

                for sug in details["suggestions"]:

                    result_lines.append(f"- Suggestion: {sug['suggestion']}")

                    result_lines.append(f"  - Workflow: {sug['workflow']}, Status: {sug['status']}")

                result_lines.append("")  # Spacer

 

            return " ".join(result_lines)

        else:

            return f"Error fetching mitigation plans: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"

    except Exception as e:

        return f"API Error: {e}"

 

 

@tool

def update_chemical_recommendation(product_name: str, workflowID: str, actionStatus: str , issueStatus: str) -> str:

    """

    Executes the mitigation plan for a given product.

    Marks recommendations as 'Completed' and quality issues as 'Resolved'.

    """

    actionStatus="Completed"

    issueStatus="Resolved"

 

    headers = {

        "Content-Type": "application/json"

    }

 

    payload = {

        "productID": product_name,

        "workflowID": workflowID,

        "actionStatus": actionStatus,

        "issueStatus": issueStatus

    }

 

    post_url = f"{BASE_URL}/updateRecommendations"

 

    try:

        response = requests.post(post_url, headers=headers, json=payload)

 

        if response.ok:

            data = response.json()

            return f"✅ Mitigation plan executed for product '{product_name}' (Workflow: {workflowID}) {data.get('value') or data.get('message')}"

        else:

            return f"❌ Failed to update recommendation: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"

 

    except Exception as e:

        return f"❌ Error invoking mitigation update: {str(e)}"

Understanding the Tools:

  1. @tool decorator: This tells LangChain that this function is a tool the agent can use
  2. Docstring: The text inside triple quotes tells the agent WHEN to use this tool. The agent reads this to decide which tool to call.
  3. get_products_with_quality_issues:
    • Makes a GET request to /QualityIssue with OData filter
    • $expand=batch includes related batch information
    • $filter gets only Open or Escalated issues
    • Returns formatted text describing each issue
  4. get_mitigation_plan:
    • Calls the custom RecommendationView we created in CAP
    • Groups recommendations by product
    • Returns structured mitigation plans with workflow IDs
  5. update_chemical_recommendation:
    • Posts to the custom updateRecommendations action
    • Updates recommendation status to "Completed"
    • Updates quality issue status to "Resolved"
    • Returns success/failure message

Key Design Pattern: All tools return strings (not objects) because the LLM needs text to understand the results.

Step 3: Creating the AI Agent (llm_agent.py)

Now let's set up the agent that uses these tools intelligently.

File: llm_agent.py

from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_openai_functions_agent

from langchain.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, SystemMessagePromptTemplate, HumanMessagePromptTemplate, MessagesPlaceholder

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI

import json

import os

 

# Load AI Core credentials

with open("ai_core.json", "r") as key_file:

    svcKey = json.load(key_file)

 

authUrl = svcKey["url"]

clientid = svcKey["clientid"]

clientsecret = svcKey["clientsecret"]

apiUrl = svcKey["serviceurls"]["AI_API_URL"]

 

# Set required environment variables

env_vars = {

    "AICORE_AUTH_URL": authUrl,

    "AICORE_CLIENT_ID": clientid,

    "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET": clientsecret,

    "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP": "default",

    "AICORE_BASE_URL": apiUrl

}

os.environ.update(env_vars)

 

# Import tools from your service

from chemical_service import (

    get_products_with_quality_issues,

    get_mitigation_plan,

    update_chemical_recommendation,

)

 

# Get AI Core proxy client and LLM

proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')

llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o-mini', proxy_client=proxy_client, temperature=0.0)

 

# Register tools

tools = [

    get_products_with_quality_issues,

    get_mitigation_plan,

    update_chemical_recommendation,

]

 

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([

    SystemMessagePromptTemplate.from_template(

        "You are a helpful assistant that uses tools to reason about chemical product batches, quality issues, and mitigation plans."

    ),

    HumanMessagePromptTemplate.from_template("{input}"),

    MessagesPlaceholder(variable_name="agent_scratchpad")  # Required by agent

])

 

# Create agent with proper prompt

agent = create_openai_functions_agent(

    llm=llm,

    tools=tools,

    prompt=prompt

)

 

# Setup agent executor

agent_executor = AgentExecutor(

    agent=agent,

    tools=tools,

    verbose=True,

    return_intermediate_steps=True,

    handle_parsing_errors=True

)

Understanding the Agent Setup:

  1. Loading AI Core Credentials:
  1. with open("ai_core.json", "r") as key_file:
  2.     svcKey = json.load(key_file)

Reads your SAP AI Core service key that you created in Part 1

  1. Setting Environment Variables:
  1. os.environ.update(env_vars)

The SAP AI Core SDK needs these environment variables to authenticate

  1. Getting the Proxy Client:
  1. proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')

This creates a connection to SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub

  1. Initializing the LLM:
  1. llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o-mini', proxy_client=proxy_client, temperature=0.0)
    • proxy_model_name='gpt-4o-mini': Uses GPT-4o-mini model (fast and cost-effective)
    • temperature=0.0: Makes responses deterministic (consistent)
  1. Creating the Prompt:
  • prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([...])
    • SystemMessage: Tells the agent its role and capabilities
    • HumanMessage: The user's input (filled in at runtime)
    • agent_scratchpad: Where the agent writes its reasoning steps
  1. Creating the Agent:
  • agent = create_openai_functions_agent(llm=llm, tools=tools, prompt=prompt)

This creates an agent that can call functions (tools)

  1. Agent Executor:
  • agent_executor = AgentExecutor(...)
    • verbose=True: Shows the agent's thinking in console (great for debugging!)
    • return_intermediate_steps=True: Returns which tools were called
    • handle_parsing_errors=True: Gracefully handles errors

Step 4: Creating the FastAPI Service (main.py)

Finally, let's create the web API that makes the agent accessible.

File: main.py

from fastapi import FastAPI, Query

from llm_agent import agent_executor

 

app = FastAPI()

 

@app.get("/")

def read_root():

    return {"message": "LangChain + CAP OData Integration API is live!"}

 

@app.get("/qualityIssue")

def chemical_quality_issues(prompt: str = Query(..., description="Natural language prompt")):

    try:

        result = agent_executor.invoke({"input": prompt})

        return {"response": result}

    except Exception as e:

        return {"error": str(e)} 

 

 

@app.get("/mitigationPlan")

def quality_issue_mitigation_plan(prompt: str = Query(..., description="Natural language prompt")):

    try:

        result = agent_executor.invoke({"input": prompt})

        return {"response": result}

    except Exception as e:

        return {"error": str(e)} 

 

 

@app.get("/executeMitigationPlan")

def execute_mitigation_plan(prompt: str = Query(..., description="Natural language prompt")):

    try:

        result = agent_executor.invoke({"input": prompt})

        return {"response": result}

    except Exception as e:

        return {"error": str(e)}

Understanding the FastAPI Endpoints:

  1. Root Endpoint (/😞
  1. @app.get("/")
  2. def read_root():

Simple health check to verify the API is running

  1. Quality Issue Endpoint (/qualityIssue😞
  1. @app.get("/qualityIssue")
  2. def chemical_quality_issues(prompt: str = Query(...)):
    • Accepts a natural language query as a parameter
    • Query(...) means this parameter is required
    • Passes the prompt to the agent executor
    • Returns the agent's response
  1. Mitigation Plan Endpoint (/mitigationPlan😞 Same pattern but for getting mitigation recommendations
  2. Execute Mitigation Endpoint (/executeMitigationPlan😞 For executing corrective actions through natural language

The Magic:

result = agent_executor.invoke({"input": prompt})

This single line:

  • Takes the user's natural language query
  • Sends it to the agent
  • Agent reads available tools
  • Agent decides which tool(s) to call
  • Agent calls the tools
  • Agent formulates a response
  • Returns structured results

Step 5: Installing Dependencies

Create requirements.txt:

fastapi==0.109.0

uvicorn[standard]==0.27.0

requests==2.31.0

langchain==0.1.0

langchain-openai==0.0.2

generative-ai-hub-sdk==1.0.0

Install everything:

# Make sure you're in your virtual environment

source venv/bin/activate  # Mac/Linux

# or

venv\Scripts\activate  # Windows

 

# Install dependencies

pip install -r requirements.txt

Step 6: Testing the Complete System

6.1 Start the CAP Backend

First terminal:

cd chemical-quality-system

cds watch

You should see:

[cds] - serving ChemicalService { path: '/odata/v4/chemical' }

[cds] - server listening on { url: 'http://localhost:4004' }

6.2 Start the FastAPI Service

Second terminal:

cd chemical-quality-agent

source venv/bin/activate

uvicorn main:app --reload --port 8000

You should see:

INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000

INFO:     Application startup complete.

6.3 Test with Your Browser

Open: http://localhost:8000/docs

This opens the interactive API documentation (automatically generated by FastAPI!).

Test Query 1: Check Quality Issues

  1. Click on /qualityIssue endpoint
  2. Click "Try it out"
  3. Enter prompt: What quality issues do we currently have?
  4. Click "Execute"

You'll see the agent:

  • Call get_products_with_quality_issues tool
  • Return formatted results

Test Query 2: Get Mitigation Plans

  1. Click on /mitigationPlan endpoint
  2. Click "Try it out"
  3. Enter prompt: Show me the mitigation plans for products with quality issues
  4. Click "Execute"

Test Query 3: Execute Mitigation

  1. Click on /executeMitigationPlan endpoint
  2. Click "Try it out"
  3. Enter prompt: Execute the mitigation plan for CHEM-B-2024 using workflow WF-001
  4. Click "Execute"

6.4 Watch the Agent Think (Verbose Mode)

In your second terminal (where FastAPI is running), you'll see the agent's reasoning:

> Entering new AgentExecutor chain...

 

Invoking: `get_products_with_quality_issues` with `{'input': ''}`

 

Product ID: CHEM-B-2024, Issue: Out-of-Spec pH, Severity: High

 

The current quality issues include:

- **Product ID:** CHEM-B-2024

- **Issue:** Out-of-Spec pH

- **Severity:** High

 

> Finished chain.

This shows you exactly what the agent is doing!

6.5 Test with cURL

# Check quality issues

curl -X GET "http://localhost:8000/qualityIssue?prompt=What%20quality%20issues%20exist%3F"

 

# Get mitigation plans

curl -X GET "http://localhost:8000/mitigationPlan?prompt=Show%20mitigation%20plans"

 

# Execute mitigation

curl -X GET "http://localhost:8000/executeMitigationPlan?prompt=Execute%20mitigation%20for%20CHEM-B-2024%20workflow%20WF-001"

6.6 Test with Python

import requests

 

# Check quality issues

response = requests.get(

    "http://localhost:8000/qualityIssue",

    params={"prompt": "What quality issues do we have?"}

)

print(response.json())

 

# Get mitigation plans

response = requests.get(

    "http://localhost:8000/mitigationPlan",

    params={"prompt": "Show me mitigation plans"}

)

print(response.json())

Understanding the Agent's Decision-Making

Let's trace what happens when you ask: "What quality issues do we have?"

Step 1: User Query Arrives

GET /qualityIssue?prompt=What quality issues do we have?

Step 2: FastAPI Receives Request

def chemical_quality_issues(prompt: str = Query(...)):

    result = agent_executor.invoke({"input": prompt})

Step 3: Agent Analyzes Query The agent (GPT-4o-mini) reads:

  • System prompt: "You help with chemical quality management"
  • User question: "What quality issues do we have?"
  • Available tools and their descriptions

Step 4: Agent Decides "This question is asking about quality issues. I should use get_products_with_quality_issues tool."

Step 5: Agent Calls Tool

get_products_with_quality_issues.invoke({"input": ""})

Step 6: Tool Executes

# Makes HTTP request

requests.get("http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/chemical/QualityIssue?...")

Step 7: Tool Returns Results

"Product ID: CHEM-B-2024, Issue: Out-of-Spec pH, Severity: High"

Step 8: Agent Formulates Response Agent reads the tool output and creates a natural language response:

"Currently, we have one quality issue:

Product CHEM-B-2024 has an Out-of-Spec pH issue with High severity."

Step 9: FastAPI Returns JSON

{

  "response": {

    "input": "What quality issues do we have?",

    "output": "Currently, we have one quality issue...",

    "intermediate_steps": [...]

  }

}

Customizing the System

Add a New Tool

Want to check sensor data? Add to chemical_service.py:

@tool

def get_sensor_readings(batch_id: str) -> str:

    """Get the latest sensor readings for a specific batch."""

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/SensorLog"

    params = {

        "$filter": f"batch/productID eq '{batch_id}'",

        "$orderby": "timestamp desc",

        "$top": 5

    }

   

    try:

        response = requests.get(url, params=params)

        if response.ok:

            logs = response.json().get("value", [])

            # Format and return

            return format_sensor_data(logs)

    except Exception as e:

        return f"Error: {e}"

Then add it to the tools list in llm_agent.py:

from chemical_service import (

    get_products_with_quality_issues,

    get_mitigation_plan,

    update_chemical_recommendation,

    get_sensor_readings  # Add this

)

 

tools = [

    get_products_with_quality_issues,

    get_mitigation_plan,

    update_chemical_recommendation,

    get_sensor_readings  # Add this

]

Customize the System Prompt

Make the agent more specific by editing the prompt in llm_agent.py:

SystemMessagePromptTemplate.from_template(

    "You are a chemical quality expert assistant specializing in pharmaceutical manufacturing. "

    "You help identify quality issues, suggest corrective actions, and execute mitigation plans. "

    "Always prioritize safety and compliance with GMP regulations."

)

Add More Endpoints

Add specialized endpoints in main.py:

@app.get("/dailyReport")

def daily_quality_report(date: str = Query(...)):

    prompt = f"Generate a quality report for {date} including all issues and their status"

    result = agent_executor.invoke({"input": prompt})

    return {"response": result}

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Issue 1: "Module not found: gen_ai_hub"

Solution:

pip install generative-ai-hub-sdk

Issue 2: "Connection refused to localhost:4004"

Solution: Make sure CAP service is running:

cd chemical-quality-system

cds watch

Issue 3: "Invalid AI Core credentials"

Solution: Verify your ai_core.json file exists and has correct format:

{

  "clientid": "...",

  "clientsecret": "...",

  "url": "...",

  "serviceurls": {

    "AI_API_URL": "..."

  }

}

Issue 4: Agent keeps calling wrong tool

Solution: Improve the tool's docstring to be more specific:

@tool

def get_products_with_quality_issues(input: str) -> str:

    """

    Use this tool ONLY when the user asks about:

    - Current quality problems

    - Which products have issues

    - Open or escalated quality concerns

   

    Do NOT use for: mitigation plans, sensor data, or historical issues

    """

Issue 5: "verbose" output not showing

Solution: Check your terminal where you ran uvicorn main:app. The verbose output appears there, not in the API response.

Performance Tips

  1. Use temperature=0.0 for consistent, deterministic responses
  2. Keep tool descriptions clear so the agent makes good decisions
  3. Cache frequent queries in your tools if needed
  4. Set max_iterations in AgentExecutor to prevent infinite loops

What We've Accomplished

In Part 3, you've successfully:

✅ Created three LangChain tools that interact with CAP backend
✅ Set up an AI agent connected to SAP AI Core
✅ Built FastAPI endpoints for natural language queries
✅ Tested the complete system end-to-end
✅ Learned how agents make decisions

Next Steps

In Part 4, we'll deploy this FastAPI service to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry so it's accessible in production, complete with:

  • Manifest.yaml configuration
  • Environment variable management
  • Cloud deployment steps
  • Production best practices

Key Takeaways

The Power of LangChain:

  • Tools let AI agents interact with external systems
  • Agents intelligently decide which tools to use
  • Natural language becomes the interface

The Beauty of This Architecture:

  • CAP Backend: Handles data and business logic
  • FastAPI: Provides web API
  • LangChain: Enables AI reasoning
  • SAP AI Core: Provides the LLM

Everything works together seamlessly!

Continue to Part 4: Deploying to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

entry.title: Building an AI-Powered Chemical Quality Management System: Part 4 - Deploying to BTP Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Introduction

Welcome to the final part of our series! You've built a complete AI-powered quality management system:

  • Part 1: SAP CAP Backend
  • Part 2: Python, FastAPI & LangChain Fundamentals
  • Part 3: Complete AI Agent Implementation

Now it's time to deploy your FastAPI agent service to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry so it's accessible in production. No more "localhost" - your AI agent will be running in the cloud!

What you'll learn:

  • How Cloud Foundry works on SAP BTP
  • Creating a production-ready manifest.yaml
  • Managing environment variables securely
  • Deploying and monitoring your application
  • Best practices for production deployments

What is SAP BTP Cloud Foundry?

Cloud Foundry is a Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) that runs on SAP Business Technology Platform. Think of it as:

  • A managed environment where you just push your code and Cloud Foundry handles the rest
  • Automatic scaling based on your application's needs
  • Built-in monitoring and logging
  • Integration with SAP services (like AI Core)

Why use Cloud Foundry for our agent?

  1. Easy deployment: One command to deploy
  2. Automatic restarts: If your app crashes, Cloud Foundry restarts it
  3. Environment management: Easy dev/test/prod separation
  4. Service binding: Automatic connection to SAP AI Core
  5. Scalability: Scale up/down based on demand

Prerequisites

Before deployment, ensure you have:

✅ SAP BTP account with Cloud Foundry enabled
✅ Cloud Foundry CLI installed
✅ Your FastAPI application from Part 3
✅ SAP AI Core service instance and credentials

Installing Cloud Foundry CLI

Windows:

# Download installer from:

# https://github.com/cloudfoundry/cli/releases

# Run the .exe installer

Mac:

brew install cloudfoundry/tap/cf-cli@8

Linux:

wget -q -O - https://packages.cloudfoundry.org/debian/cli.cloudfoundry.org.key | sudo apt-key add -

echo "deb https://packages.cloudfoundry.org/debian stable main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/cloudfoundry-cli.list

sudo apt-get update

sudo apt-get install cf8-cli

Verify installation:

cf --version

Step 1: Preparing Your Application for Cloud Foundry

1.1 Restructure for Cloud Deployment

Cloud Foundry needs a slightly different structure. Let's reorganize:               

├── Chemical_Service_agent/                       # NEW: Our Python agent for Cloud Foundry

│   ├── chemical_service.py

│   ├── llm_agent.py

│   ├── btpaiagent.py           # NEW: Renamed from main.py

│   ├── requirements.txt

│   ├── manifest.yml            # NEW: Cloud Foundry configuration

│   ├── runtime.txt             # NEW: Python version

│   └── Procfile                # NEW: Start command

└── ai_core.json

1.2 Create btpaiagent.py

Rename and modify main.py to btpaiagent.py with Cloud Foundry compatibility:

Key Changes for Cloud Foundry:

  1. PORT environment variable: Cloud Foundry assigns a random port
  1. port = int(os.getenv("PORT", 8080))
  1. Health endpoint: Cloud Foundry checks if app is healthy
  1. @app.get("/health")
  1. VCAP_APPLICATION: Cloud Foundry provides app metadata
  1. os.getenv("VCAP_APPLICATION", "local")

1.3 Update llm_agent.py for Cloud Environment

File: agent/llm_agent.py

from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_openai_functions_agent

from langchain.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, SystemMessagePromptTemplate, HumanMessagePromptTemplate, MessagesPlaceholder

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI

import os

 

# Load AI Core credentials from environment variables (Cloud Foundry)

authUrl = os.getenv("AICORE_AUTH_URL")

clientid = os.getenv("AICORE_CLIENT_ID")

clientsecret = os.getenv("AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET")

apiUrl = os.getenv("AICORE_BASE_URL")

resourceGroup = os.getenv("AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP", "default")

 

# Validate credentials

if not all([authUrl, clientid, clientsecret, apiUrl]):

    raise ValueError("Missing required AI Core environment variables")

 

# Set environment variables for SDK

env_vars = {

    "AICORE_AUTH_URL": authUrl,

    "AICORE_CLIENT_ID": clientid,

    "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET": clientsecret,

    "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP": resourceGroup,

    "AICORE_BASE_URL": apiUrl

}

os.environ.update(env_vars)

 

# Import tools

from chemical_service import (

    get_products_with_quality_issues,

    get_mitigation_plan,

    update_chemical_recommendation,

)

 

# Get AI Core proxy client and LLM

proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')

llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o-mini', proxy_client=proxy_client, temperature=0.0)

 

# Register tools

tools = [

    get_products_with_quality_issues,

    get_mitigation_plan,

    update_chemical_recommendation,

]

 

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([

    SystemMessagePromptTemplate.from_template(

        "You are a helpful assistant that uses tools to reason about chemical product batches, quality issues, and mitigation plans."

    ),

    HumanMessagePromptTemplate.from_template("{input}"),

    MessagesPlaceholder(variable_name="agent_scratchpad")

])

 

# Create agent

agent = create_openai_functions_agent(

    llm=llm,

    tools=tools,

    prompt=prompt

)

 

# Setup agent executor

agent_executor = AgentExecutor(

    agent=agent,

    tools=tools,

    verbose=True,

    return_intermediate_steps=True,

    handle_parsing_errors=True

)

Key Change: Uses environment variables instead of ai_core.json file.

1.4 Update chemical_service.py for Production

File: agent/chemical_service.py

from langchain.tools import tool

import requests

import os

 

# Get CAP backend URL from environment or use default

BASE_URL = os.getenv("CAP_BACKEND_URL", "http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/chemical")

 

@tool

def get_products_with_quality_issues(input: str) -> str:

    """

    Get all product batches with quality issues (Open or Escalated).

    """

    url = f"{BASE_URL}/QualityIssue?$expand=batch&$filter=status eq 'Open' or status eq 'Escalated'"

    try:

        response = requests.get(url)

        if response.ok:

            issues = response.json().get("value", [])

            if not issues:

                return "No open or escalated quality issues found."

            result = " ".join([

                f"Product ID: {issue['batch']['productID']}, Issue: {issue['issueType']}, Severity: {issue.get('severity', 'N/A')}"

                for issue in issues

            ])

            return result

        return f"Error fetching issues: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"

    except Exception as e:

        return f"Exception occurred: {e}"

 

 

@tool

def get_mitigation_plan(input: str) -> str:

    """

    Fetch mitigation plans for product batches with quality issues.

    Filters based on 'Open' or 'Escalated' statuses and provides structured suggestions.

    """

    try:

        response = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/RecommendationView")

        if response.ok:

            recommendations = response.json().get("value", [])

            filtered = [rec for rec in recommendations if rec["status"] in ["Open", "Escalated"]]

 

            if not filtered:

                return "No open or escalated mitigation plans found."

 

            result_lines = []

            grouped = {}

            for rec in filtered:

                product = rec["productID"]

                if product not in grouped:

                    grouped[product] = {

                        "severity": rec["severity"],

                        "suggestions": []

                    }

                grouped[product]["suggestions"].append({

                    "suggestion": rec["suggestion"],

                    "workflow": rec["triggeredWorkflowID"],

                    "status": rec["status"]

                })

 

            for product, details in grouped.items():

                result_lines.append(f"### {product} (Severity: {details['severity']})")

                for sug in details["suggestions"]:

                    result_lines.append(f"- Suggestion: {sug['suggestion']}")

                    result_lines.append(f"  - Workflow: {sug['workflow']}, Status: {sug['status']}")

                result_lines.append("")

 

            return " ".join(result_lines)

        else:

            return f"Error fetching mitigation plans: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"

    except Exception as e:

        return f"API Error: {e}"

 

 

@tool

def update_chemical_recommendation(product_name: str, workflowID: str, actionStatus: str, issueStatus: str) -> str:

    """

    Executes the mitigation plan for a given product.

    Marks recommendations as 'Completed' and quality issues as 'Resolved'.

    """

    actionStatus = "Completed"

    issueStatus = "Resolved"

 

    headers = {

        "Content-Type": "application/json"

    }

 

    payload = {

        "productID": product_name,

        "workflowID": workflowID,

        "actionStatus": actionStatus,

        "issueStatus": issueStatus

    }

 

    post_url = f"{BASE_URL}/updateRecommendations"

 

    try:

        response = requests.post(post_url, headers=headers, json=payload)

 

        if response.ok:

            data = response.json()

            return f"✅ Mitigation plan executed for product '{product_name}' (Workflow: {workflowID}) {data.get('value') or data.get('message')}"

        else:

            return f"❌ Failed to update recommendation: {response.status_code} - {response.text}"

 

    except Exception as e:

        return f"❌ Error invoking mitigation update: {str(e)}"

Key Addition: Configurable BASE_URL from environment variable.

Step 2: Creating Cloud Foundry Configuration Files

2.1 Create manifest.yml

This is the most important file - it tells Cloud Foundry how to run your app.

File: agent/manifest.yml

---

applications:

- name: btpaiagent

  memory: 256M

  disk_quota: 1G

  instances: 1

  buildpacks:

  - https://github.com/cloudfoundry/python-buildpack.git

  command: python btpaiagent.py

  random-route: false

  health-check-type: http

  health-check-http-endpoint: /health

  timeout: 180

  env:

    AICORE_BASE_URL: https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com

    AICORE_CLIENT_ID: your-client-id

    AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET: your-client-secret

    AICORE_AUTH_URL: https://your-subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

    AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP: default

    CAP_BACKEND_URL: https://your-cap-backend.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/odata/v4/chemical

Understanding manifest.yml:

name: btpaiagent

  • Your application name in Cloud Foundry
  • Will be used in the URL: btpaiagent.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

memory: 256M

disk_quota: 1G

  • Memory: RAM allocated to your app (256MB is enough for FastAPI)
  • Disk: Storage space for your app and dependencies

instances: 1

  • Number of app instances to run
  • Start with 1, scale up later if needed

buildpacks:

- https://github.com/cloudfoundry/python-buildpack.git

  • Tells Cloud Foundry this is a Python application
  • Buildpack installs Python and dependencies automatically

command: python btpaiagent.py

  • The command to start your application
  • Runs btpaiagent.py which starts uvicorn

health-check-type: http

health-check-http-endpoint: /health

  • Cloud Foundry checks /health endpoint to verify app is running
  • If unhealthy, Cloud Foundry restarts the app

timeout: 180

  • Maximum time (seconds) for app to start
  • AI Core connection can take time, so 180s is safe

env:

  • Environment variables for your application
  • DO NOT commit real credentials to git!

2.2 Create requirements.txt

File: agent/requirements.txt

fastapi==0.109.0

uvicorn[standard]==0.27.0

requests==2.31.0

langchain==0.1.0

langchain-openai==0.0.2

generative-ai-hub-sdk==1.0.0

2.3 Create runtime.txt

Specifies Python version for Cloud Foundry.

File: agent/runtime.txt

python-3.11.x

2.4 Create Procfile (Optional)

Alternative way to specify start command.

File: agent/Procfile

web: python btpaiagent.py

Step 3: Securing Your Credentials

NEVER commit real credentials to git! Let's use secure practices.

3.1 Create .gitignore

File: agent/.gitignore

# Credentials

ai_core.json

manifest.yml

 

# Python

__pycache__/

*.pyc

*.pyo

venv/

.env

 

# IDE

.vscode/

.idea/

3.2 Create manifest-template.yml

File: agent/manifest-template.yml

---

applications:

- name: btpaiagent

  memory: 256M

  disk_quota: 1G

  instances: 1

  buildpacks:

  - https://github.com/cloudfoundry/python-buildpack.git

  command: python btpaiagent.py

  random-route: false

  health-check-type: http

  health-check-http-endpoint: /health

  timeout: 180

  env:

    AICORE_BASE_URL: <YOUR_AI_CORE_BASE_URL>

    AICORE_CLIENT_ID: <YOUR_CLIENT_ID>

    AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET: <YOUR_CLIENT_SECRET>

    AICORE_AUTH_URL: <YOUR_AUTH_URL>

    AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP: default

    CAP_BACKEND_URL: <YOUR_CAP_BACKEND_URL>

3.3 Fill in Your Credentials

Copy the template and add real values:

cd agent

cp manifest-template.yml manifest.yml

# Now edit manifest.yml with your actual credentials

Get your credentials from ai_core.json:

{

  "clientid": "sb-xxxxx",           → AICORE_CLIENT_ID

  "clientsecret": "xxxxx=",         → AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET

  "url": "https://...",             → AICORE_AUTH_URL

  "serviceurls": {

    "AI_API_URL": "https://..."     → AICORE_BASE_URL

  }

}

Step 4: Deploying to Cloud Foundry

4.1 Login to Cloud Foundry

# Login to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

cf login -a https://api.cf.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

 

# You'll be prompted for:

# - Email

# - Password

# - Org (organization)

# - Space (dev, test, prod)

Example:

API endpoint: https://api.cf.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

Email: your.email@company.com

Password: ********

 

Select an org:

  1. my-company-org

 

Org: 1

 

Select a space:

  1. dev
  2. test
  3. prod

 

Space: 1

 

Targeted org my-company-org

Targeted space dev

4.2 Verify Your Target

cf target

You should see:

API endpoint:   https://api.cf.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

API version:    3.x.x

User:           your.email@company.com

Org:            my-company-org

Space:          dev

4.3 Push Your Application

# Navigate to agent directory

cd agent

 

# Deploy to Cloud Foundry

cf push

What happens during cf push:

  1. Upload: Your code is uploaded to Cloud Foundry
  2. Staging: Python buildpack is downloaded and installed
  3. Dependencies: pip install -r requirements.txt runs
  4. Start: Your app starts with python btpaiagent.py
  5. Health Check: Cloud Foundry checks /health endpoint
  6. Route: A URL is created for your app

You'll see output like:

Pushing app btpaiagent to org my-company-org / space dev as your.email@company.com...

Uploading files...

 8.52 MiB / 8.52 MiB [======================] 100.00% 2s

 

Staging app...

Cell 1234 creating container for instance 5678

Creating app with these attributes...

+ name:       btpaiagent

  path:       /path/to/agent

+ memory:     256M

+ disk:       1G

+ instances:  1

 

Staging process started ...

...

Staging complete

Uploading droplet...

 

Waiting for app to start...

 

name:              btpaiagent

requested state:   started

instances:         1/1

usage:             256M x 1 instances

routes:            btpaiagent.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

last uploaded:     Thu 06 Nov 14:32:15 UTC 2025

stack:             cflinuxfs3

buildpacks:        python

 

     state     since                  cpu    memory         disk

#0   running   2025-11-06T14:32:30Z   0.0%   45M of 256M    512M of 1G

Success! Your app is running at: https://btpaiagent.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

4.4 Test Your Deployed App

# Test health endpoint

curl https://btpaiagent.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/health

 

# Test quality issues

curl "https://btpaiagent.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/qualityIssue?prompt=What%20quality%20issues%20exist?"

 

# View in browser

# Open: https://btpaiagent.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/docs

Step 5: Troubleshooting Cloud Foundry Deployments

Issue 1: App Fails to Start

Symptoms:

App instance crashed

Start unsuccessful

Solution:

# Check logs

cf logs btpaiagent --recent

 

# Common causes:

# 1. Missing dependencies - check requirements.txt

# 2. Wrong Python version - check runtime.txt

# 3. Port binding error - ensure app uses PORT env variable

# 4. Missing environment variables - check manifest.yml

Fix port binding:

# In btpaiagent.py

port = int(os.getenv("PORT", 8080))  # Cloud Foundry sets PORT

uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=port)

Issue 2: Health Check Failing

Symptoms:

App has crashed

Health check never passed

Solution:

# Increase timeout in manifest.yml

timeout: 240  # Increase from 180 to 240 seconds

 

# Check health endpoint locally

cf logs btpaiagent --recent | grep health

Verify health endpoint:

@app.get("/health")

def health_check():

    return {"status": "UP"}  # Must return 200 status

Issue 3: Environment Variables Not Working

Symptoms:

Missing required AI Core environment variables

KeyError: 'AICORE_BASE_URL'

Solution:

# Check environment variables

cf env btpaiagent

 

# Set individual variable

cf set-env btpaiagent AICORE_BASE_URL "https://..."

 

# Restart to apply

cf restart btpaiagent

Issue 4: Out of Memory

Symptoms:

Instance exceeded memory limit

App crashed with exit status 137

Solution:

# Increase memory allocation

cf scale btpaiagent -m 512M

 

# Check memory usage

cf app btpaiagent

Issue 5: Connection to CAP Backend Fails

Symptoms:

Error fetching issues: Connection refused

API Error: timeout

Solution:

# Check CAP backend URL

cf env btpaiagent | grep CAP_BACKEND_URL

 

# Update if wrong

cf set-env btpaiagent CAP_BACKEND_URL "https://your-cap-backend.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/odata/v4/chemical"

 

# Restart

cf restart btpaiagent

Test connectivity:

# Add to btpaiagent.py

@app.get("/test-connection")

def test_cap_connection():

    try:

        import requests

        url = os.getenv("CAP_BACKEND_URL")

        response = requests.get(f"{url}/ProductBatch", timeout=5)

        return {

            "cap_backend": url,

            "status": response.status_code,

            "reachable": response.ok

        }

    except Exception as e:

        return {

            "cap_backend": url,

            "error": str(e),

            "reachable": False

        }

Issue 6: AI Core Authentication Fails

Symptoms:

401 Unauthorized

Invalid client credentials

Solution:

# Verify credentials

cf env btpaiagent | grep AICORE

 

# Update credentials from ai_core.json

cf set-env btpaiagent AICORE_CLIENT_ID "sb-xxxxx"

cf set-env btpaiagent AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET "xxxxx="

cf set-env btpaiagent AICORE_AUTH_URL "https://..."

 

# Restart

cf restart btpaiagent

Issue 7: Slow Performance

Symptoms:

  • Requests taking >30 seconds
  • Timeouts

Solution:

# Scale up instances for load balancing

cf scale btpaiagent -i 3

 

# Increase memory for better performance

cf scale btpaiagent -m 512M

 

# Check app metrics

cf app btpaiagent

Add caching to tools:

from functools import lru_cache

import time

 

cache_timestamp = {}

 

@tool

def get_products_with_quality_issues(input: str) -> str:

    """Get all product batches with quality issues (Open or Escalated)."""

    cache_key = "quality_issues"

    current_time = time.time()

   

    # Use cached result if less than 5 minutes old

    if cache_key in cache_timestamp:

        if current_time - cache_timestamp[cache_key] < 300:

            return cached_get_issues()

   

    # Fetch fresh data

    result = fetch_quality_issues()

    cache_timestamp[cache_key] = current_time

    return result

Step 6: Example Production Manifest

Here's a production-ready manifest.yml with all best practices:

File: agent/manifest-production.yml

---

applications:

- name: btpaiagent-prod

  memory: 512M

  disk_quota: 1G

  instances: 2

  buildpacks:

  - https://github.com/cloudfoundry/python-buildpack.git

  command: python btpaiagent.py

  random-route: false

  health-check-type: http

  health-check-http-endpoint: /health

  timeout: 180

  env:

    PYTHONUNBUFFERED: true

    LOG_LEVEL: INFO

    AICORE_BASE_URL: https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com

    AICORE_CLIENT_ID: your-production-client-id

    AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET: your-production-client-secret

    AICORE_AUTH_URL: https://your-subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

    AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP: production

    CAP_BACKEND_URL: https://cap-prod.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/odata/v4/chemical

  services:

  - my-app-logs

  - my-autoscaler

  routes:

  - route: btpaiagent-prod.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

Key production settings:

  1. instances: 2 - High availability with 2 instances
  2. memory: 512M - More memory for production load
  3. services - Bound to logging and autoscaling
  4. PYTHONUNBUFFERED: true - See logs in real-time
  5. Separate resource group - "production" for AI Core

 

 

 

What You've Accomplished

Congratulations! 🎉 You've successfully:

✅ Part 1: Built a robust SAP CAP backend for chemical quality management
✅ Part 2: Learned Python, FastAPI, and LangChain fundamentals
✅ Part 3: Created an intelligent AI agent with custom tools
✅ Part 4: Deployed the agent to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

Your complete system:

  • SAP CAP Backend managing production data
  • AI Agent powered by LangChain and SAP AI Core
  • FastAPI Service deployed on Cloud Foundry
  • Natural Language Interface for quality management

Resources

SAP BTP Cloud Foundry:

  • Official Documentation
  • Cloud Foundry CLI Reference

SAP AI Core:

  • AI Core Documentation
  • AI Launchpad

Python & FastAPI:

  • FastAPI Deployment
  • LangChain Documentation

Community:

  • SAP Community
  • Stack Overflow - SAP BTP

Conclusion

You've completed a comprehensive journey from concept to production deployment! Your AI-powered chemical quality management system is now running in the cloud, accessible to users, and ready for real-world use.

Remember:

  • Always test in dev/test before production
  • Monitor your app regularly
  • Keep dependencies updated
  • Document your deployment process
  • Use proper security practices

Thank you for following this series! You now have the skills to build and deploy AI-powered applications on SAP BTP.

Happy deploying! 🚀

entry.title: Запис вебінару: Технології та інновації як рушійні сили зростання бізнесу
entry.content:
Не встигли потрапити на міжнародну онлайн-лекцію "Технології та інновації як рушійні сили зростання бізнесу"?

— не біда!


На каналі Європейської Бізнес Асоціації (EBA) на вас чекає запис вебінару, де експерт Йожек Грушковняк аналізує технології як головний інструмент розвитку бізнесу; розглядає глобальні виклики, зокрема демографічні зміни, економічну фрагментацію та екологічні ризики, що змушують компанії шукати нові шляхи для зростання. Особлива увага приділяється цифровій трансформації та впровадженню систем ERP, які дозволяють оптимізувати ланцюги постачання та фінансові операції.

Щоб дізнатися про це більше — переходьте за посиланням.

entry.title: I Asked 50 SAP Professionals What Scares Them About AI. Here's What They Said.
entry.content:
How This All Started

Picture this. I am sitting in a room full of SAP consultants, project managers, functional leads, and business analysts at a community meetup last quarter. Someone casually drops the words “SAP AI” into the conversation and the entire energy in the room shifts.

Not with excitement. With something closer to anxiety.

That moment stayed with me. So I did something about it. Over the next few weeks I reached out to 50 SAP professionals across industries, experience levels, and geographies. I asked them one simple question:

What honestly scares you about AI in SAP?

No filters. No corporate answers. Just real people telling me real things.

What came back was fascinating, uncomfortable, validating, and hopeful all at once. Let me share it with you exactly as I heard it.

Before We Get Into It, A Quick Question For You

Poll: What is your gut feeling when you hear “SAP AI” right now?

  • Honestly a little scared
  • Curious but uncertain
  • Indifferent, still watching
  • Genuinely excited

Pick one. We will revisit your answer at the end. You might surprise yourself.

Fear #1: Am I Going to Become Irrelevant?

This came up more than anything else. 31 out of 50 professionals mentioned job security in some form. Not always directly. Sometimes it sounded like this:

“I have spent 14 years mastering SAP FI. If AI can do what I do in seconds, what exactly am I bringing to the table?”

Or this:

“My client literally asked me last month if they still needed my team once Joule was fully live. That question kept me up at night.”

If you felt this way and said it out loud, that took courage. The fear is not irrational. But history shows that every major enterprise technology shift created more specialized roles than it eliminated. SAP itself was once feared as a job killer. Today it supports millions of professionals globally.

AI will not replace the SAP professional who understands the business deeply. It will replace the SAP professional who refuses to evolve.

The question is not whether AI will change your role. It already is. The question is whether you will shape that change or be shaped by it.

A Moment to Reflect

Poll: How worried are you personally about AI affecting your SAP career?

  • Very worried
  • A little worried
  • Not worried, I see the opportunity
  • Not sure yet

Whatever you picked is valid. You are not alone.

Fear #2: What Happens When AI Gets It Wrong?

This concern came mostly from technical professionals. 24 out of 50 raised accuracy and accountability risks. One senior SAP architect said:

“My worry is not that AI will be wrong. My worry is that it will be wrong confidently. And nobody will check it because everyone assumes the machine is right.”

We have all seen what happens when a financial posting goes wrong in SAP. Month end chaos. Audit questions. Stressed finance teams. Now imagine that at AI speed and scale.

This is a real concern. The answer is not to avoid AI. The answer is governance inside your SAP landscape. Human checkpoints. Confidence thresholds. Exception workflows. These are foundational controls.

Fear #3: Our Data Is a Mess and Nobody Wants to Say It

22 out of 50 professionals mentioned data quality as their hidden fear. One project manager said:

“Everyone in leadership is excited about SAP AI. Nobody wants to be the person who says our master data is a disaster.”

AI amplifies whatever already exists in your SAP system. Clean data gets smarter. Messy data gets messier faster. Years of inconsistent master records cannot be covered by an AI layer.

Before AI deployment, do the foundational work. Run data quality audits. Fix master data governance. Strengthen controls. It is not glamorous, but it works.

Quick Check In

Poll: How would you describe your SAP data quality today?

  • Clean and governed
  • Mixed quality
  • Messy
  • Not sure

Fear #4: Change Management Will Kill This Before It Starts

19 out of 50 professionals focused on people risk, not technology risk. One change consultant shared:

“I have seen strong SAP programs fail because users never trusted the system. AI is even more personal. If users feel replaced, adoption drops quietly.”

Resistance is often silent. Users override suggestions. Avoid tools. Work around the system. Adoption drops and leadership asks why.

If you are leading SAP AI initiatives, your first meeting should be with end users, not vendors.

Fear #5: Leadership Thinks AI Is a Magic Button

17 out of 50 professionals expressed leadership expectation gaps. One said:

“Our CEO declared we would be AI first this year. We are not even fully on S/4HANA yet.”

There is a gap between boardroom AI excitement and SAP reality. Urgency without readiness creates burnout. Realism without excitement creates inertia. Strong programs balance both.

Implementers must educate upward with clarity and courage.

The Other Side of Fear

After asking about fears, I asked what gives them hope. The tone shifted:

“If AI removes manual reconciliations, I can focus on analysis.”

“Junior consultants adapt quickly. That gives me confidence.”

“I resisted Fiori at first too. Now I cannot imagine working without it.”

Every SAP disruption felt uncomfortable before it felt normal. This one is no different.

entry.title: Improving the SAP Community together, one comment at a time
entry.content:
As a Developer Advocate, I have the privilege of working with some amazing colleagues in that team, but also in the wider Developer and Community Relations organisation. For the size of the community, it's a small group, and the folks are working hard to keep the wheels on the bus and rolling in the right direction. But they can only do so much. In this post, I want to highlight a challenge for us all, and how we can, again, collectively, tackle it together.

The challenge

I'm talking about content quality, which to a greater or lesser degree has for a long time been a topic of debate both in the questions area and also the blog post area. My relationship with the SAP Community platform goes back to day 1, with SDN.

Actually, it goes back to well before that - creating and running the first online SAP community in 1995, and co-creating the SAP Developer Network has caused me to never stop caring for and worrying about those platforms, the people, and the content.

Blog post quality

The quality of some blog posts on the SAP Community platform could be better. I know it. You know it. We all know it. The number of people like you and me who worry about this sort of thing is far outweighed by the larger number of folks that make up the entire community.

Over time various folks have attempted to help educate folks so that they create better content. Here are some posts from me on this topic:

  • Help us to help you - share your code (2014)
  • Help us to help you - good questions beget good answers (2019)
  • Monday morning thoughts: a good blog post (2018)
  • Monday morning thoughts: on starting blogging (2018)

These attempts have worked, but only to a certain extent. Not only that, but we have AI content generation to contend with too. While used appropriately, and with thought, this approach can produce great results. But it can also produce poor content, especially when the human component in this equation is not entirely engaged with the purpose and process. Moreover, the ease with which poor content can be produced is astounding. And so the problem has got worse over time, and I can only see that progression accelerating.

The SAP Community belongs to us

Like you, seeing blog posts that don't belong makes me sad and frustrated. So, what should we do in this situation?

Report important issues to the moderators

Well, first, there's a decision to be made. Does the blog post break the important parts of the Rules Of Engagement? What are the important parts? Well, a bit of common sense will help. Does it defame, injure or harass? Is the content illegal? Report it to the moderators.

Feel empowered to call other issues out yourself

In other cases, don't report it. Use your voice publicly, by attaching a comment to the post, explaining briefly what you consider inappropriate, or just plain incorrect. Encourage your fellow SAP Community members to upvote your comment if they agree.

Use the power of the collective, the power of the shared heart, to highlight, educate, and yes, if you want to think of it in this way, to shame the author into adjusting or removing the post and changing their ways.

This is how society works. And the SAP Community platform is an online society. So feel empowered to treat it as one. Call the person out. Gently, politely, firmly, however you see fit. You be the judge - we're all adults here. Don't treat it as a kindergarten and go to the teacher whenever you dislike something.

Why? Because the teachers are in short supply. The moderators behind the scenes are working flat out on an ever increasing load of reports. Some that I would definitely class as important, but many, many others that I'd class as trivial and inappropriate.

Celebrate good posts

Calling out bad posts like this is important. Upvoting such call-out comments is also important.

But as well as calling out bad posts, remember to celebrate good ones by adding comments of praise or encouragement. Or just upvote them. That is the least you can do, it only takes a second.

The power of filtering and natural selection

Calling out poor content by leaving a comment helps educate, even gently admonish, if needed. But upvoting and adding multiple positive comments helps even more, not only to highlight good content, but also to feed the filter mechanisms that can surface content that you want to see, controlled in part by you.

Final thoughts

Thanks for reading this far. Here's a TL;DR (yes, I know it should go at the top, but think of it as the call to action instead).

Stop reporting poor quality content, and similar transgressions. Call the authors out yourself. Everyone is a member, and everyone is a moderator. And if we moderate ourselves with respect, honesty and firmness, we just might turn this giant sea-going cargo ship towards a better quality future for the community platform that belongs to all of us.

Originally published on qmacro.org

entry.title: Close Offboarding Processes automatically using Integration Center
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post, we will walk through how to automatically close offboarding processes in SAP SuccessFactors using Integration Center. Specifically, we will configure a scheduled integration that updates processes based on the employee’s termination date. By the end of this guide, you will understand how to set up the integration, map the required fields, and ensure the process status is correctly updated.

This configuration is useful for us, in the sense that often required tasks are not all completed on the platform and therefore the job responsible for closing the processes does not work, requiring manual cancellation.

1. Choose the Integration Type

The first step is to choose the integration type. In this case, we will use the Scheduled Trigger type and select SuccessFactors as both the source and destination system.

2. Select the Start Entity

For the start entity, search for ONB2Process.

3. Map the Process ID and the default values

In my case, I set the default values for Process Status and Task Status to "Completed". However, this configuration also works with other status values, depending on your requirement.

Important note: The operation must be set to Update/Merge.

Next, we need to map the Process ID. To do this, click on "Set as associated field" and search for Process ID from Process.

4. Define the Logic

In our scenario, we needed to close offboarding processes when the termination date was 30 days in the past, so we used the target date from process that corresponds to termination date :

5. Schedule and Test

After completing the configuration, you only need to schedule the job and test it. 🙂

The expected result is:

  • Process Status: Completed

  • Task Status: Closed

 

Optional: Cancelling Instead of Completing

If instead of completing the process you want to cancel it, you must also map the Offboarding Cancellation Reason field:

Conclusion

In this post, we demonstrated how to configure a scheduled integration in SAP SuccessFactors to automatically complete or cancel offboarding processes. By selecting the correct integration type, mapping the required fields, and applying appropriate date logic, you can fully automate offboarding closure when the required tasks have not been completed. This approach helps ensure data accuracy, reduces manual effort, and keeps your offboarding workflows aligned with business requirements.
